text: The selective sequestration of carbon dioxide from industrial flue gas remains a significant challenge, primarily due to the competitive adsorption of water vapor which typically diminishes material efficiency. In this study, we demonstrate the precise molecular engineering of the metal-organic framework IRMOF-74-III to achieve high-performance CO₂ capture under humid conditions. By employing a versatile synthetic strategy involving protected amine linkers and subsequent post-synthetic deprotection, a series of functionalized structures—incorporating -CH₃, -NH₂, -CH₂NHBoc, -CH₂NMeBoc, -CH₂NH₂, and -CH₂NHMe groups—were prepared and characterized. Solid-state ¹³C NMR spectroscopy confirmed that the aliphatic primary (-CH₂NH₂) and secondary (-CH₂NHMe) amines chemically bind CO₂ through the formation of carbamic species. Adsorption isotherms and dynamic breakthrough experiments revealed that the primary amine-functionalized framework, IRMOF-74-III-CH₂NH₂, exhibits a significant CO₂ capacity of 3.2 mmol g⁻¹ and maintains its performance in gas streams with 65% relative humidity. Notably, the material showed identical breakthrough times (610 ± 10 s g⁻¹) for both dry and wet nitrogen streams without structural degradation. These results highlight the effectiveness of integrating specific covalent functionality within large-pore MOFs to overcome water competition in practical carbon capture applications.
